title: Vintage Camper Napkin Holder
summary: A whimsical vintage-camper-shaped napkin holder cut from scrap wood, with a dowel tongue jack and drilled wheel details.
description:
This is a novelty-shape project first and a napkin holder second — the whole point is the silhouette, cut from a printed pattern rather than measured out board by board. Scrap wood is genuinely appropriate here since the piece is small and gets fully painted, so grain and species don't matter the way they would on a stained piece.

The dowel **"tongue jack"** isn't just decorative — it's what holds the front of the camper level on a table, exactly like a real travel trailer's jack holds its front end level when unhitched.

Drilled wheel details, cut with a hole saw on a drill press, are what sell the vintage-camper look at a glance — plain edges would read as an abstract shape rather than a specific object.

tools.note: Or a scroll saw for the curved camper body.
Cuts clean circular wheel details with a hole saw.

steps.title: Transfer the pattern
steps.body: Print the camper pattern at the size you want the finished holder and transfer it to your scrap stock — this is a silhouette project, so the pattern is the plan and there is no board-by-board measuring to do. If the holder has two matching sides, transfer the shape once and either trace the second from the first or stack the two blanks and cut them together, so the pair comes out identical. Scrap is genuinely the right material here since the whole piece gets painted, but pick something flat and free of knots along the curved cut lines.

steps.title: Cut the body and base
steps.body: Cut the curved camper body on the jigsaw, staying just outside the pattern line and sanding back to it rather than trying to hit the line with the blade — a jigsaw wanders on tight curves and a silhouette lives or dies on its outline. If you stacked two blanks, keep them clamped or taped together through the whole cut so both sides match exactly. Then cut the base to length on the miter saw; its width sets how many napkins the holder takes, so hold your napkins against it before you cut. Cut the base square at both ends so the assembled holder sits flat.

steps.title: Add the tongue jack
steps.body: Drill a 3/8" hole for the dowel tongue jack at the front underside, angled slightly forward the way a real trailer's jack leans, and set the depth so the hole does not break through the opposite face. Cut the dowel so it holds the front of the camper level on a flat table with the base sitting down — that is the jack's actual job here, not decoration, so test the length dry against the assembled body before committing. Glue it in and let it set before handling.

steps.title: Drill the wheel details
steps.body: Cut the wheel circles with a hole saw in the drill press, which keeps the cut perpendicular and the circle clean in a way a handheld drill will not. Drill only partway through so each circle reads as a recessed tire rather than a hole punched in the camper; set the depth stop on a test piece in the same stock first. If you are cutting the wheels as separate discs to apply on top instead, back the workpiece with scrap so the exit side does not blow out. These recesses are what make the silhouette read as a specific vintage trailer rather than an abstract shape.

steps.title: Assemble and finish
steps.body: Glue and Glue the joint and clamp until set. Add any separate tire pieces the same way. Sand every cut edge and soften the corners, then paint in a retro two-tone scheme — the paint is doing as much work as the sawing on a novelty piece like this, so prime first and cut the color break cleanly with tape.
